metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the current SSM parameter path of the cookie-signing secret, whether it is imported at its existing path or migrated to a `/rebar/prod/` path, and how the Lambda config is updated (or not) so no value rotation occurs.
findings.finding: The cookie-signing SSM SecureString secret (part of the auth_host SSO stack) is presumably stored under a `/snap-demo/*` namespace path (matching the `project=snap-demo` tag context), not under `/rebar/prod/*`. The existing `ssm.tf` `for_each` loop only covers `/rebar/prod/*` paths and uses a `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` guard. The plan does not state the current parameter name/path for this secret, whether a new `/rebar/prod/` path will be created (requiring value migration and a Lambda config update) or the original path imported as-is. Without this, the plan's SC-2 (clean plan, no secret rotation) cannot be verified.
findings.location: Context section — 'cookie-signing SSM secret' / Success Criteria SC-2 and SC-3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly which IAM identity (role ARN or credential source) has write access to `snap-demo-tfstate-896586841071` for the `terraform state rm` step, and confirm it is still active; add this as a precondition in the child 'terraform adoption' story.
findings.finding: The plan states resources will be 'released from snap's shared/terraform.tfstate' but does not specify the auth/IAM preconditions required to write to the `snap-demo-tfstate-896586841071` S3 bucket. rebar's current terraform execution identity (rebar-terraform-plan role) is ReadOnlyAccess-scoped and cannot perform `terraform state rm` (which calls S3 PutObject on the snap state bucket). The plan is silent on which IAM identity holds write access to snap's dead state, and whether that identity is still active post-decommission.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'The re-homed resources are removed from snap's shared/terraform.tfstate (single-owner)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to child 7246 (or a shared post-landing step) requiring the terraform-drift CI workflow to pass on main after both children land.
findings.finding: Parent acceptance criterion 'terraform-drift CI on main is green after the change lands' is not explicitly covered by either child's acceptance criteria.
findings.location: child 7246-bd9e-58a3-41fd (Adopt snap shared resources) and child 6725-32e9-2cf0-4114 (Docs) — neither child's AC set
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 7246's AC: '[operator-attested] The terraform-drift CI workflow on main passes green after both children land (evidence: link to passing workflow run).'

findings.finding: Parent acceptance criterion 'terraform-drift CI on main is green after the change lands' is not covered by any child. Child 7246's ACs cover import, plan, and apply but do not include a post-merge CI-green assertion. Child 6725 is docs-only. No sibling covers this criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 7246 AC: '[ ] [operator-attested] terraform-drift CI workflow on main passes after the merged change (evidence: green CI run URL recorded as a comment).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit steps in child 7246 for retrieving the live random_password plaintext values (via aws ssm get-parameter --with-decryption) and the exact terraform import invocation format for random_password resources.
findings.finding: Child 7246 does not specify how to correctly import `random_password` resources with live values, leaving a partial-without-remainder coverage gap on the parent's 'no secret-value rotation' criterion for this specific resource type.
findings.location: child 7246-bd9e-58a3-41fd — What section: 'import the two random_password resources with their LIVE values (read from SSM / the Lambda env)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 7246's What: 'For each random_password resource: (1) retrieve plaintext via `aws ssm get-parameter --name /auth-solutions/<param> --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value --output text`; (2) run `terraform import random_password.<name> <plaintext>`; (3) confirm plan shows result = <value> unchanged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the import+apply+retag+snap-state-removal sequence, specifying (at minimum): the safe ordering of steps, a pre-import snapshot of both states, and the operator procedure to restore snap's shared state if rebar's adoption fails before completion.
findings.finding: The plan describes an irreversible, multi-step live operation (terraform import → apply/retag → removal from snap's shared state) but provides no explicit rollback or back-out procedure. If the sequence fails mid-way — e.g., resources are imported into rebar's state but the apply fails, or the snap state removal runs before rebar's apply is confirmed clean — the 18 resources could be orphaned from both states or left in a partially-tagged inconsistent state with no documented recovery path. The plan acknowledges the risk of snap's dead state being destroyed, but never specifies what to do if rebar's adoption fails.
findings.location: ## Context (OIDC dependency paragraph) and ## Success Criteria (second bullet: clean plan)
findings.suggested_fix: Insert a 'Rollback Plan' section: (1) Before any import, snapshot both state files (`terraform state pull > rebar-pre-import.tfstate` and equivalent for snap). (2) Run import and apply against rebar BEFORE touching snap's state. (3) Only remove from snap's state after `terraform plan` in rebar reports zero changes. (4) If rebar apply fails, run `terraform state rm` for each imported resource in rebar and treat snap's state as still authoritative. Document these steps as operator-attested requirements in the child story.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: Destructive/irreversible operation(s) with no stated safeguard.
findings.suggested_fix: State the safeguard (backup, dry-run, reversible migration, rollback) or justify the irreversibility explicitly (T4).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of each auth_host public endpoint (CloudFront distribution, API GW v2, Lambda invocation URL) in the plan — state public-with-rationale, authenticated-only, or internal-only — and confirm whether API GW has an authorizer or origin-restriction that prevents direct invocation bypassing CloudFront.
findings.finding: The auth_host SSO stack (CloudFront distribution, API Gateway v2, Lambda) is a public-internet-reachable surface being re-homed, but the plan never declares its access posture (public-with-rationale / authenticated-with-roles / internal-only). Per OWASP ASVS V1 and the rubric, an undeclared access posture on a boundary-crossing path is itself the failure.
findings.location: Plan body — 'auth_host domain-wide SSO stack (API GW v2 + CloudFront + Lambda + Route53 A/AAAA + cookie-signing SSM secret)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph 'Security contract' subsection to the adoption story: (1) state whether the CloudFront distribution is the sole entry point and API GW/Lambda are not publicly invocable directly; (2) confirm the API GW has a resource policy or the Lambda has a function URL policy restricting callers to the CloudFront distribution OAC; (3) label access class as 'domain-SSO — authenticated via cookie, public DNS but not publicly triggerable'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consolidate the iam.tf edits (stale comment fix + OIDC ARN rewire) into a single child (preferably 7246, since it owns the terraform change) and remove the iam.tf file_impact from 6725, OR explicitly specify that 6725 depends-on/rebases-on 7246's iam.tf change.
findings.finding: Both child 6725 and child 7246 claim edits to `infra/terraform/iam.tf` without coordination, creating a scope overlap and merge-conflict risk on a single file.
findings.location: child 6725-32e9-2cf0-4114 (What: 'Fix the stale comment in infra/terraform/iam.tf') and child 7246-bd9e-58a3-41fd (file_impact: 'infra/terraform/iam.tf — rewire drift role to reference OIDC .arn')
findings.suggested_fix: Move the iam.tf stale comment fix into child 7246 (it is a terraform-file edit that belongs with the terraform adoption work). Update child 6725's scope to exclude iam.tf edits, since the comment becomes accurate only after 7246's changes are applied anyway.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-flight step (or a documented confirmation) that enumerates any IAM policies, SCPs, or other state files referencing `project=snap-demo` on the 18 resources before the retag is applied, and acknowledge there are no active dependents on that tag value.
findings.finding: The auth_host SSO stack is described as 'domain-wide' but the plan does not explicitly confirm that no other active consumers (other apps, teams, or tag-based IAM policies) depend on the `project=snap-demo` tag before retagging, leaving a silent breakage risk unacknowledged.
findings.location: Context section / Success Criteria: 'Adopted resources carry `Project = rebar`, not `project = snap-demo`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the child story 'terraform adoption + live import/apply/retag', add a pre-apply checklist item: run `aws resourcegroupstaggingapi get-resources --tag-filters Key=project,Values=snap-demo` to enumerate all tagged resources and cross-check any IAM/SCP tag-condition policies in account 896586841071 before applying the retag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sub-step in the child 'terraform adoption' story that explicitly accounts for CloudFront (and API GW v2 / Lambda) HCL schema validation: specify that the operator will run `aws cloudfront get-distribution` / `aws apigatewayv2 get-api` to capture the live config, generate matching HCL, and perform a `terraform plan` dry-run against the imported state before declaring SC2 satisfied.
findings.finding: The plan's Success Criterion 2 ('terraform plan is clean — no destroy/replace of the adopted live resources') is load-bearing and depends on the executor writing HCL that exactly matches the live CloudFront distribution's schema before import. CloudFront distributions in AWS provider v5 have a large, version-sensitive schema (cache behaviours, origin configs, function associations, viewer certificate blocks, etc.); any attribute mismatch post-import produces plan diffs or in-place mutations. The plan does not acknowledge this schema-matching challenge or provide a mitigation (e.g., generate HCL from the live resource via 'terraform import' + iterative plan-fix cycles, or use 'aws cloudfront get-distribution' output to pre-populate HCL). This is an unverified capability gap for the specific resource type in a codebase that currently has zero CloudFront HCL.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'terraform plan in infra/terraform is clean (no destroy/replace of the adopted live resources)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the child story's task list, add: 'Before apply, run `terraform import <resource_address> <live_id>` for each CloudFront/APIGW/Lambda resource, then immediately run `terraform plan`; iterate on HCL until plan shows 0 changes before proceeding to retag apply.' Reference `aws cloudfront get-distribution --id <id>` and `aws apigatewayv2 get-api --api-id <id>` as the ground-truth sources for schema population.

findings.finding: No child verifies that snap's shared state file remains valid (no orphaned resource entries, no accidental destroy-on-next-apply risk) after `terraform state rm` removes the 18 adopted resources. The parent's SC4 only requires removal; it does not require confirmation that snap's remaining state is consistent. If snap's terraform configuration still references the removed resources, a future accidental `terraform apply` on snap's dead state could attempt to recreate or destroy them.
findings.location: child 7246-bd9e-58a3-41fd — 'What' section: 'terraform state rm the adopted resources from snap's infra/shared state'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 7246: after state rm, either delete snap's shared HCL declarations for the adopted resources or add a note in the snap repo README that these resources are now managed by rebar. Record the post-removal terraform plan output for snap's state as evidence (should show 0 planned changes or be marked destroyed/archived).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all 18 resource addresses (as `module.x.aws_y.z` terraform identifiers) in the plan so the success criterion is self-validating and the executor has a definitive import checklist.
findings.finding: The plan states '18 shared resources' are to be adopted but does not enumerate them by Terraform resource address. An executor must independently discover the exact set from snap's dead state file, creating a risk of miscount or scope mismatch that the success criterion ('All 18 shared resources defined and tracked') cannot self-validate without an authoritative list.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or list in the Context or a new 'Resource Inventory' section mapping each of the 18 resources to its Terraform resource address and AWS resource ID/ARN (e.g. `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github` → `arn:aws:iam::896586841071:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com`). This makes the import checklist explicit and the success criterion directly verifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider"` HCL block that must be added to `infra/terraform/iam.tf` (or a new file) as the import target, and update the trust-policy `identifiers` in the same block to reference `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.<name>.arn` instead of the current hardcoded ARN string.
findings.finding: The plan mandates `terraform import` of the OIDC provider into rebar's state but no `resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider"` HCL block exists in infra/terraform/** — `terraform import` requires a target resource address to already be declared in HCL, so the import will fail without it. Additionally, `iam.tf` currently embeds the provider ARN as a hardcoded literal string in the trust policy principal rather than referencing an adopted resource, meaning the two artefacts (HCL block + the existing ARN string) must be reconciled in the same commit or the plan will show a no-op import target.
findings.location: Context section / Success Criteria SC-1 / infra/terraform/iam.tf line 147
findings.suggested_fix: Add a resource block such as `resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "github_actions" { url = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" client_id_list = ["sts.amazonaws.com"] thumbprint_list = ["<current-thumbprint>"] tags = { Project = "rebar" } }` to iam.tf, update the trust policy principal to use `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github_actions.arn`, then import with `terraform import aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github_actions token.actions.githubusercontent.com`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate and include the ACM DNS validation Route53 CNAME records in the 18-resource adoption list, specify their import addresses, and ensure they are removed from snap's state in the same operation as the cert itself.
findings.finding: The wildcard ACM certificate (`*.solutions.navateam.com`) is issued via DNS validation, which creates Route53 CNAME records as proof-of-ownership. Those validation records likely also live in snap's dead state. The plan adopts the cert resource but does not address co-adoption of its DNS validation records: if those CNAMEs are removed from snap's state without being imported into rebar's state, the ACM resource will show stale `domain_validation_options` output, and any future cert renewal or re-validation will fail silently.
findings.location: Context section — wildcard cert + auth_host SSO stack description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the adoption story, declare (a) the exact SSM parameter path(s) the Lambda execution role is permitted to read (must be scoped to the cookie-signing key only, not wildcarded), and (b) a post-adoption secret-rotation plan or explicit acceptance of static secret with documented rationale.
findings.finding: The cookie-signing SSM secret's post-adoption IAM access scope is undeclared. The plan says the secret will not be rotated (to avoid churn) but does not state which IAM principal(s) are permitted to read it, whether the Lambda execution role's SSM access will be scoped to its specific parameter path, or whether any rotation cadence is planned.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria — 'no secret-value rotation'; plan body — 'cookie-signing SSM secret'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a task to the 'terraform adoption' child story to enumerate and review the auth_host Lambda execution role's IAM permissions, confirm they are scoped to the minimum required (SSM read for the signing key only, CloudWatch Logs write), and re-scope or document any deviation.
findings.finding: The Lambda execution role for the auth_host SSO stack is not addressed in the plan. Physical resource names are preserved (snap-demo-*), meaning the existing Lambda role is implicitly inherited, but the plan commits to no review or re-scoping of that role's permissions as part of the re-home.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Physical resource names are snap-demo-*; renaming forces destroy/recreate … so names are PRESERVED and only ownership + the project=snap-demo tag are moved to rebar'

findings.finding: Child 7246 plans to import `random_password` resources with their live values, but the hashicorp/random provider's `random_password` resource does not support `terraform import` — there is no importable identity for a generated password. This structurally guarantees that applying the config will regenerate the passwords (secret rotation), directly contradicting both the parent's SC2 ('no secret-value rotation') and child 7246's own operator-attested AC ('no secret-value change').
findings.location: child 7246-bd9e-58a3-41fd — 'What' section: 'import the two random_password resources with their LIVE values'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove random_password resources for the cookie-signing secret; instead declare an aws_ssm_parameter data source reading the existing /auth-solutions/* SSM value and reference it directly. This avoids the import problem entirely and matches the SSM-as-source-of-truth pattern already used in rebar.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
